Interview keyword: Bottom of stack is the root, minimal transport, test added.

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you read an OOABAP call stack in ST22?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: ST22 mein, OO call stack program entry point se failing line tak class aur method calls ki chain dikhata hai. Main isse bottom-up padhta hoon: bottom entry wahaan hai jahan runtime error actually hua — class name, method name, aur line number. Phir main SE80 mein us class par navigate karta hoon aur us line ka examination karta hoon. Upar wali calling chain mujhe batati hai ki execution wahaan kaise pahuncha — jo identify karne mein help karta hai ki bug failing method mein khud hai ya calling class dwara pass kiye invalid input mein. Factory patterns ke liye, main check karta hoon ki failure se pehle factory correct concrete class select kar raha tha ya nahi.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: In ST22, the OO call stack shows the chain of class and method calls from the program entry point down to the failing line. I read it bottom-up: the bottom entry is where the runtime error actually occurred — the class name, method name, and line number. I then navigate to that class in SE80 and examine that line. The calling chain above tells me how execution arrived there — which helps identify whether the bug is in the failing method itself or in invalid input passed by the calling class. For factory patterns, I check if the factory was selecting the correct concrete class before the failure.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: ST22 mein 'Active Calls/Events' section click karo — full OO call stack dikhega. Har entry pe double-click → source code us line pe open hoga. Eclipse mein Stack Frames sidebar bhi same info deta hai during live debugging session.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Fixing the top-level class in the call stack rather than the actual failing class at the bottom. The top-level class is often just the entry point — it may be perfectly correct. The bug is where execution failed, not where it started.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" ST22 Call Stack Example (read bottom-up):
" 1. ZCL_ORDER_REPORT→START          ← entry point (top)
" 2.   ZCL_ORDER_SERVICE→PROCESS     ← orchestration
" 3.     ZCL_PRICING_ENGINE→GET_PRICE ← pricing called
" 4.       ZCL_GST_CALC→CALCULATE    ← ROOT CAUSE (bottom)
"           Error: Division by zero line 23

" Fix target: ZCL_GST_CALC line 23
" NOT ZCL_ORDER_REPORT (entry point)
" Navigate: SE80 → ZCL_GST_CALC → CALCULATE method → line 23

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you debug an OOABAP class in the ABAP Debugger?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: OOABAP debugging ke liye, main specific class method ke andar breakpoints set karta hoon jisme mujhe shak hai ki yeh fail ho raha hai — ya toh Eclipse breakpoint ya temporary BREAK-POINT statement ke through. Jab execution rukti hai, main object reference ko expand karne aur saare attributes inspect karne ke liye Variables panel use karta hoon, private wale bhi sahit. Step Into mujhe called methods mein execution follow karne deta hai. Factory pattern debugging ke liye, main factory method mein ek breakpoint set karta hoon aur watch karta hoon ki kaunsa concrete class instantiate ho raha hai. Observer debugging ke liye, main notification ke point par observer list attribute inspect karta hoon yeh dekhne ke liye ki kitne observers registered hain.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: For OOABAP debugging, I set breakpoints inside the specific class method I suspect is failing — either via Eclipse breakpoint or temporary BREAK-POINT statement. When execution stops, I use the Variables panel to expand the object reference and inspect all attributes, including private ones. Step Into lets me follow the execution into called methods. For factory pattern debugging, I set a breakpoint in the factory method and watch which concrete class is being instantiated. For observer debugging, I inspect the observer list attribute to see how many observers are registered at the point of notification.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Production mein debugging: /H in transaction field → debugger activates for next call. In production, minimize breakpoint time — other users' work processes can be affected if you hold a breakpoint too long. Use watchpoints (conditional breakpoints) to stop only on specific conditions rather than every call.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Setting breakpoints at the entry point of a long chain and stepping through 200 lines to find the issue. Instead, set the breakpoint at the suspected failing class directly — identified from ST22 call stack. This reduces debugging time from hours to minutes.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" ABAP Debugger OO Tips:
" Set breakpoint in specific method:
CLASS zcl_gst_calculator IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD zif_tax_calculator~calculate.
    BREAK-POINT.  " temporary — remove after debug
    rv_tax = iv_amount * '0.18'.
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

" Eclipse: click gutter on the line → red dot = breakpoint
" F5 = Step Into (follow into called method)
" F6 = Step Over (skip method call)
" F7 = Step Return (run until method returns)
" Variables panel: expand lo_object → see all attributes
" Watch expression: lo_obj->mv_amount to monitor value

followupAnswerBank.question: What causes a singleton to fail in production under load?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Production load ke under singleton failures ke typically do causes hote hain. Pehla, lazy initialization race condition: multiple ABAP work processes simultaneously IS NOT BOUND check karte hain aur dono isse true paate hain, dono ek new instance banate hain — do singletons exist karte hain, pattern violate karte hue. Solution: singleton ko class constructor event mein initialize karo jise SAP ek baar per ABAP session run hone ka ensure karta hai, ya ek mutex use karo. Doosra, constructor mein silent exception swallowing: agar constructor ek exception raise karta hai jo catch aur suppress ho jaata hai, mo_instance kabhi set nahi hota — har subsequent call re-initialization attempt karti hai aur phir fail hoti hai. Solution: constructor exceptions ko propagate hone do aur re-raising se pehle logging add karo.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Singleton failures under production load typically have two causes. First, lazy initialization race condition: multiple ABAP work processes simultaneously check IS NOT BOUND and both find it true, both create a new instance — two singletons exist, violating the pattern. Solution: initialize the singleton in the class constructor event which SAP ensures runs once per ABAP session, or use a mutex. Second, silent exception swallowing in the constructor: if the constructor raises an exception that is caught and suppressed, mo_instance is never set — every subsequent call attempts re-initialization and fails again. Solution: let constructor exceptions propagate and add logging before re-raising.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: ABAP mein work process per ABAP session mein class static data independent hota hai — ek work process ka singleton doosre work process ka singleton nahi hota. Isliye ABAP mein singleton 'per session' concept hai, not system-wide. Yeh SAP-specific behavior interview mein mention karo — shows deep ABAP knowledge.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Assuming one singleton instance exists system-wide in ABAP. Each ABAP work process has its own CLASS-DATA memory — singleton is per-session, not per-system. This is important for configuration caching: if config changes, all active sessions must be invalidated (typically by restarting them or using shared memory objects).
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Singleton failure: silent exception in constructor
CLASS zcl_config IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD get_instance.
    IF mo_instance IS NOT BOUND.
      TRY.
        mo_instance = NEW zcl_config( ).
      CATCH cx_no_authority.  " BAD: silently swallowed!
        " mo_instance stays unbound → loop!
      ENDTRY.
    ENDIF.
    ro_inst = mo_instance.
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.
" Fix: let exception propagate or log before re-raise:
" CATCH cx_no_authority INTO DATA(lx_ex).
"   " Log it
"   RAISE EXCEPTION lx_ex.  " propagate - caller must handle

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you find which observer failed when observer pattern is not working?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Jab observer pattern expected results produce nahi kar raha, main ek teen-step investigation follow karta hoon. Pehle, main observer registration check karta hoon: ABAP Debugger mein, main subject ke mt_observers internal table attribute ko inspect karta hoon — kya yeh empty hai? Agar empty hai, registration call execute nahi hua. Main register_observer method par ek breakpoint set karta hoon confirm karne ke liye. Doosre, agar observers registered hain, main notification loop check karta hoon: kya ek observer ke andar exception silently catch ho raha hai, subsequent observers ko notify hone se rokte hue? Teesre, main har observer ke kisi bhi logging ke liye SLG1 check karta hoon — log entries ki presence ya absence mujhe batati hai ki kaunse observers execute huye aur kaunse nahi.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: When observer pattern is not producing expected results, I follow a three-step investigation. First, I check observer registration: in the ABAP Debugger, I inspect the mt_observers internal table attribute of the subject — is it empty? If empty, the registration call did not execute. I set a breakpoint at the register_observer method to confirm. Second, if observers are registered, I check the notification loop: is an exception inside an observer being silently caught, preventing subsequent observers from being notified? Third, I check SLG1 for any logging each observer writes — presence or absence of log entries tells me which observers executed and which did not.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Observer pattern robustness tip: notification loop should catch per-observer exceptions and continue notifying remaining observers rather than aborting the entire notification on first failure. This is a defensive design that prevents one faulty observer from blocking all others — important in production where partial notification is better than no notification.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Assuming a registered observer is always called. An exception in the notification loop without proper error handling can silently skip some observers. Always add try-catch per observer in the notification loop and log any observer failures.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Robust observer notification loop:
METHOD notify_observers.
  LOOP AT mt_observers INTO DATA(lo_observer).
    TRY.
      lo_observer->on_event( iv_event_type ).
    CATCH cx_root INTO DATA(lx_ex).
      " Log failure but continue to next observer
      CALL FUNCTION 'BAL_LOG_MSG_ADD'
        EXPORTING i_log_handle = mo_log_handle
                  i_s_msg = VALUE bal_s_msg(
                    msgty = 'E'
                    msgv1 = lx_ex->get_text( ) ).
      " Do NOT re-raise — other observers must still run
    ENDTRY.
  ENDLOOP.
ENDMETHOD.

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you handle a factory pattern failure in production escalation?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Production mein factory pattern failures typically teen errors mein se ek ke roop mein present hote hain: object reference not bound jab factory ek unhandled input type ke liye null return karta hai, CX_SY_CREATE_OBJECT_ERROR jab factory ek aisi class reference karta hai jo abhi transport nahi hui, ya wrong behavior jab factory ek incorrect concrete class select karta hai. Main ST22 error type aur call stack se identify karta hoon ki yeh kaunsa case hai. Null reference ke liye: main factory mein ek defensive check add karta hoon — agar koi bhi known type match nahi karta, null return karne ke bajaye ek meaningful exception raise karo. Missing class ke liye: main check karta hoon ki corresponding class transport mein hai ya nahi aur kya transport PRD mein complete hua.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Factory pattern failures in production typically present as one of three errors: object reference not bound when the factory returns null for an unhandled input type, CX_SY_CREATE_OBJECT_ERROR when the factory references a class not yet transported, or wrong behavior when the factory selects an incorrect concrete class. I identify which case it is from the ST22 error type and call stack. For null reference: I add a defensive check in the factory — if none of the known types match, raise a meaningful exception rather than returning null. For missing class: I check if the corresponding class is in the transport and if transport was completed in PRD.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Factory defensive coding: always add a default ELSE or OTHERS branch in the factory SWITCH/COND that raises a descriptive exception — 'Unknown type X requested from factory' — rather than returning NULL silently. NULL return from factory causes a cryptic 'object reference not bound' dump far away from the actual factory code, making debugging much harder.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Factory that returns null for unrecognized input types without raising an exception. The caller gets a null reference and fails with an unhelpful 'object reference not bound' error that points to the caller, not the factory. Always raise a clear exception in the factory for unknown input.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Factory with defensive error handling:
CLASS zcl_tax_factory IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD create.
    ro_calc = SWITCH #( iv_country
      WHEN 'IN' THEN NEW zcl_gst_calculator( )
      WHEN 'DE' THEN NEW zcl_vat_calculator( )
      WHEN 'US' THEN NEW zcl_us_tax_calculator( )
      ELSE THROW zcx_unknown_country(  " descriptive exception!
             iv_country = iv_country ) ).
    " Never return null — caller gets clear error
    " ST22 will point HERE not to caller line
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you escalate an OOABAP issue when you are not the original developer?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Ek escalation ke dauraan doosre developer ka OO code debug karte waqt, main ek quick architectural scan se shuru karta hoon: hierarchy samajhne ke liye SE80 class browser, entry points aur callers identify karne ke liye key classes par Where-Used, aur intended behavior samajhne ke liye unit tests padhna. Phir main failing class aur method locate karne ke liye ST22 call stack use karta hoon — mujhe poori codebase samajhne ki zaroorat nahi, sirf woh specific chain jo fail hui. Agar unit tests exist karte hain, main unhe run karta hoon yeh dekhne ke liye ki kaunse fail hote hain — failing tests aksar immediately broken behavior par point karte hain. Main original developer ko available hone par involve karta hoon, par P1 escalation hone par wait nahi karta.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: When debugging another developer's OO code during an escalation, I start with a quick architectural scan: SE80 class browser to understand the hierarchy, Where-Used on key classes to identify entry points and callers, and reading unit tests to understand intended behavior. I then use the ST22 call stack to locate the failing class and method — I do not need to understand the entire codebase, only the specific chain that failed. If unit tests exist, I run them to see which ones fail — failing tests often immediately point to the broken behavior. I involve the original developer if available, but do not wait if the escalation is P1.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Ek practical approach: failing class ka unit test run karo (ATC ya Eclipse). Agar test fail ho raha hai — the test itself tells you what the expected behavior was and what the actual behavior is. This is faster than manual code reading for understanding developer intent.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Spending too long trying to understand the complete OO architecture before making any progress on the escalation. In P1, focus on the failing class and method identified from ST22 — understand just enough context to make a safe fix. Full code understanding can come after the escalation is resolved.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Quick OO code orientation in escalation:
" Step 1: SE80 → Class Browser → ZCL_FAILING_CLASS
"   → check DEFINITION: what interfaces implemented?
"   → check IMPLEMENTATION: how many methods?
" Step 2: SE80 → Where-Used → find callers
"   → entry points: which program/class calls this?
" Step 3: Read unit tests (ltc_* class same file)
"   → tests document expected behavior clearly
" Step 4: ST22 call stack → failing method → line
"   → read 10 lines around failing line only
" Step 5: Fix minimum scope → test → transport

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you communicate an OOABAP bug fix to business stakeholders during escalation?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: OOABAP escalations ke dauraan, main stakeholder communication ke liye technical findings ko business language mein translate karta hoon. 'ZCL_GST_CALCULATOR mein calculate method mein division by zero tha' kehne ke bajaye, main bolta hoon 'tax calculation module zero quantity wale orders process karte waqt fail hua — ek validation check missing tha.' Main business communications mein kabhi class names, method names, ya design patterns mention nahi karta. Update template hai: business terms mein kya fail hua, simple language mein kyun fail hua, fix karne ke liye hum kya kar rahe hain, aur kab resolve hoga. Technical details sirf internal incident ticket mein developer reference ke liye jaate hain.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: During OOABAP escalations, I translate technical findings into business language for stakeholder communication. Instead of 'ZCL_GST_CALCULATOR had a division by zero in the calculate method,' I say 'the tax calculation module failed when processing orders with zero quantity — a validation check was missing.' I never mention class names, method names, or design patterns in business communications. The update template is: what failed in business terms, why it failed in simple language, what we are doing to fix it, and when it will be resolved. Technical details go only in the internal incident ticket for developer reference.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Good stakeholder communication during P1 builds trust even when the fix is not ready. Saying 'We have identified the root cause in the tax calculation module and the fix is being prepared for emergency deployment — ETA 45 minutes' gives business confidence that progress is being made. Silence during P1 is the worst outcome — even negative updates are better than no updates.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Using developer language directly with business stakeholders: 'The factory pattern returned a null reference when the country code was not in the switch statement.' Stakeholders do not understand this and it reduces confidence in the support team. Always translate to business domain language.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Stakeholder communication template:
" Subject: [P1 UPDATE] Order Tax Calculation Issue
"
" Business impact: Tax calculation failing on zero-quantity
"   orders. Approximately 12 orders affected since 09:00.
"
" Root cause (simple language):
"   Tax module was missing a check for zero-quantity orders.
"   This caused the calculation to fail unexpectedly.
"
" Fix status:
"   Fix developed and tested (DEV + QAS completed).
"   Emergency transport approval obtained from Manager X.
"   PRD deployment in progress — ETA: 14:30
"
" Workaround: Affected orders can be reprocessed after
"   14:30 once fix is deployed.
" Next update: 14:30 with deployment confirmation.

followupAnswerBank.question: What preventive monitoring can you add after an OOABAP escalation?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Ek OOABAP escalation resolve karne ke baad, main same class ke issue ko rokne ya early-detect karne ke liye targeted monitoring add karta hoon. Factory pattern ke liye: SLG1 mein received type aur instantiated class log karo — koi bhi unknown type dump cause karne se pehle immediately log mein appear hota hai. Observer pattern ke liye: startup par observer registration count log karo — zero observers ek event fire hone se pehle early warning hai. Exception handling ke liye: ensure karo ki saare catch blocks swallowing ya re-raising se pehle SLG1 mein log karein — silent exceptions visible ho jaati hain. Unit tests ke liye: escalation cause karne wale exact edge case ko cover karne wala ek test case add karo — ensure karta hai ki regression kabhi reoccur na ho.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: After resolving an OOABAP escalation, I add targeted monitoring to prevent or early-detect the same class of issue. For factory pattern: log the type received and class instantiated in SLG1 — any unknown type immediately appears in the log before causing a dump. For observer pattern: log observer registration count at startup — zero observers is an early warning before any event fires. For exception handling: ensure all catch blocks log to SLG1 before swallowing or re-raising — silent exceptions become visible. For unit tests: add a test case covering the exact edge case that caused the escalation — ensures regression never reoccurs.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: SLG1 logging in factory pattern: one log entry per factory call showing input type and output class. This is typically a few milliseconds overhead but provides invaluable forensic data when the factory misbehaves. In a production post-mortem, this log can immediately show 'factory was receiving type X but no class was registered for X' — root cause in seconds instead of hours.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Closing the escalation without adding any preventive measure and hoping the same issue does not recur. Without prevention, the same edge case will hit again — possibly during an even more critical business window. Always add at minimum one monitoring or validation improvement as part of escalation closure.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Post-escalation monitoring additions:
" 1. Factory logging (add after escalation):
CLASS zcl_tax_factory IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD create.
    " Log for monitoring
    MESSAGE |Factory: country={ iv_country }| TYPE 'I'
      INTO DATA(lv_dummy).
    " Existing logic below...
    ro_calc = SWITCH #( iv_country
      WHEN 'IN' THEN NEW zcl_gst_calculator( )
      ELSE THROW zcx_unknown_country( iv_country ) ).
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

" 2. Unit test for the edge case that caused escalation:
METHOD test_zero_quantity_does_not_fail.  " new test
  DATA(lo_calc) = NEW zcl_gst_calculator( ).
  DATA(lv_tax) = lo_calc->calculate( iv_amount = 0 ).
  cl_abap_unit_assert=>assert_equals( act = lv_tax  exp = 0 ).
ENDMETHOD.

ooabap-034.easyUnderstanding: Simple words mein samjho: FS matlab Functional Specification — jo business team likhti hai. TS matlab Technical Specification — jo ABAP developer likhta hai. FS mein likha hota hai: 'User ek button click karega aur system us material ke liye best price calculate karega considering quantity discounts, customer category, aur company code.' TS mein developer likhta hai: 'ZCL_PRICING_ENGINE class, interface ZIF_PRICING implement karega, get_best_price method, MARA se material check, KONV se condition records, Factory pattern se correct pricing algorithm select karo, unit test required, transport request DEVK900XXX.' FS mein kya, kyun, kiske liye. TS mein kaise, kaunsa class, kaunsa table, kaunsi design, kaunsi pattern. OOABAP ke context mein FS-to-TS conversion extra important hai kyunki: ek baar TS mein class design commit ho gayi — interface define ho gayi — phir badalna expensive hota hai. Interface change matlab sab implementing classes change. Isliye TS mein zyada time spend karo OO design pe — sahi interface define karo, sahi class boundaries set karo, sahi patterns identify karo. Baad mein development fast aur stable hoti hai.
ooabap-034.hinglishMasterExplanation:
OOABAP ke liye FS se TS conversion ka step-by-step framework:

1. FS READING — OO LENS SE:
FS padho aur OO objects identify karo:
- Kaunse business entities hain? Har entity ek potential class hai. 'Customer', 'Pricing Rule', 'Tax Calculator', 'Order Processor' — yeh sab classes ban sakte hain.
- Kaunse behaviors hain? Har behavior ek method hai. 'Calculate price', 'Validate customer', 'Apply discount' — yeh methods hain.
- Kaunse business rules vary karte hain? Variation points Strategy ya Factory pattern indicate karte hain. 'Agar customer India ka hai toh GST, Germany ka hai toh VAT' → Factory pattern needed.
- Kaunsi cheezein events trigger karti hain? 'Jab order save ho toh email jaaye, print ho, workflow start ho' → Observer pattern needed.

2. INTERFACE DESIGN — SABSE PAHLE:
TS mein pehla section: interface definitions. Interface ek baar sahi define ho gayi toh sab baaki cheezein stable rehti hain. Interface design checklist:
- Method signatures: importing, exporting, changing, returning parameters clearly define karo — types explicit likho
- One method: ek kaam kare — SRP at method level
- Interface segregation: ek bada interface todke chhote focused interfaces banao agar clients sirf subset use karenge
- Naming convention: ZIF_* prefix, verb-noun naming for methods (get_price, validate_customer, process_order)

3. CLASS HIERARCHY DESIGN:
Interface ke baad class structure design karo:
- Abstract base class: shared implementation code jab Template Method pattern use karna ho
- Concrete implementing classes: ek per business variant (country, document type, customer category)
- Factory class: concrete class selection logic yahan
- Orchestration/Service class: workflow coordination yahan
- Each class: FINAL by default unless extension point intentional

4. DEPENDENCY MAPPING:
TS mein explicitly likho: kaunsi class kaunsi class pe depend karti hai. Dependency graph banana optional nahi — OO code mein yeh mandatory documentation hai. Circular dependencies flag karo aur resolve karo — extract shared abstraction.

5. UNIT TEST DESIGN IN TS:
TS mein unit test section:
- Kaunsi test classes likhni hain (ltc_*)
- Kaunse scenarios cover karne hain: happy path, edge cases, error paths
- Mock objects ki zarurat: kaunsi dependencies mock banani hain
- Expected test data: input aur expected output per test scenario

6. TRANSPORT OBJECTS LIST:
TS mein explicitly list karo sab objects jo transport mein jayenge:
- Interfaces (ZIF_*)
- Abstract classes (ZCL_*_BASE)
- Concrete classes (ZCL_*)
- Factory classes (ZCL_*_FACTORY)
- Exception classes (ZCX_*)
- Message classes (if needed)
- Custom tables (if data persistence needed)

7. FUNCTIONAL CONSULTANT VALIDATION:
TS draft banane ke baad functional consultant ko review krao — woh check karega: 'Is technical design se mere FS mein describe kiye gaye sab business rules implement honge?' Yeh cross-validation gaps prevent karta hai jo rework cause karte hain.
ooabap-034.interviewMeKyaBolnaHai:
Interview Me Bolna Hai:
'For OOABAP FS-to-TS conversion, I start by reading the FS through an OO lens: identifying business entities as potential classes, behaviors as methods, variation points that indicate Factory or Strategy pattern, and event-driven requirements that indicate Observer pattern. I then design interfaces first — method signatures, parameter types, and naming conventions following Clean ABAP standards. Class hierarchy comes next: abstract base for shared logic, concrete implementing classes per variant, factory for selection, service class for orchestration. The TS explicitly documents dependency relationships to identify circular dependencies early. I include a unit test design section covering test classes, test scenarios, and mock requirements. Finally, I list all transport objects so the full scope is clear before development begins. I validate the TS with the functional consultant before starting development — this prevents rework more than anything else.'

Hinglish tip: 'Interview mein ek specific FS-to-TS example do. FS: multi-currency tax calculation for India and Germany. TS: ZIF_TAX_CALCULATOR interface with calculate method. ZCL_GST_CALCULATOR for India, ZCL_VAT_CALCULATOR for Germany. ZCL_TAX_FACTORY decides by company code. ZCX_UNKNOWN_COUNTRY exception. Unit tests: ltc_gst_calculator, ltc_vat_calculator, ltc_tax_factory. Transport objects: five classes. Yeh level of detail dikhata hai ki tum FS ko actual OO architecture mein convert kar sakte ho — bahut strong answer.'
ooabap-034.codeExamples:
" FS-to-TS Conversion: Multi-currency Tax Example
" FS: Calculate tax for India (GST 18%) and Germany (VAT 19%)
" TS OUTPUT — OO Design:

" Step 1: Interface (design first)
INTERFACE zif_tax_calculator.
  METHODS calculate
    IMPORTING iv_amount   TYPE netwr
              iv_currency TYPE waers
    RETURNING VALUE(rv_tax) TYPE netwr.
ENDINTERFACE.

" Step 2: Concrete implementations per country variant
CLASS zcl_gst_calculator DEFINITION PUBLIC FINAL.
  PUBLIC SECTION.
    INTERFACES zif_tax_calculator.
ENDCLASS.

" Step 3: Factory for selection logic
CLASS zcl_tax_factory DEFINITION PUBLIC FINAL.
  PUBLIC SECTION.
    CLASS-METHODS create
      IMPORTING iv_bukrs      TYPE bukrs
      RETURNING VALUE(ro_tax) TYPE REF TO zif_tax_calculator.
ENDCLASS.

" TS Section: Transport Objects
" ZIF_TAX_CALCULATOR, ZCL_GST_CALCULATOR,
" ZCL_VAT_CALCULATOR, ZCL_TAX_FACTORY, ZCX_UNKNOWN_BUKRS
ooabap-034.interviewScriptEnglish: For OOABAP FS-to-TS conversion, I start by reading the FS through an OO lens: identifying business entities as potential classes, behaviors as methods, variation points that indicate Factory or Strategy pattern, and event-driven requirements that indicate Observer pattern. I then design interfaces first — method signatures, parameter types, and naming conventions following Clean ABAP standards. Class hierarchy comes next: abstract base for shared logic, concrete implementing classes per variant, factory for selection, service class for orchestration. The TS explicitly documents dependency relationships to identify circular dependencies early. I include a unit test design section covering test classes, test scenarios, and mock requirements. Finally, I list all transport objects so the full scope is clear before development begins. I validate the TS with the functional consultant before starting development — this prevents rework more than anything else.
ooabap-034.interviewScriptHinglish: OOABAP FS-to-TS conversion ke liye, main FS ko OO lens ke through padhne se start karta hoon: business entities ko potential classes ki tarah identify karta hoon, behaviors ko methods ki tarah, variation points jo Factory ya Strategy pattern indicate karte hain, aur event-driven requirements jo Observer pattern indicate karte hain. Phir main pehle interfaces design karta hoon — method signatures, parameter types, aur Clean ABAP standards follow karne wali naming conventions. Class hierarchy next aati hai: shared logic ke liye abstract base, per variant concrete implementing classes, selection ke liye factory, orchestration ke liye service class. TS explicitly dependency relationships document karta hai taaki circular dependencies jaldi identify ho. Main ek unit test design section include karta hoon jo test classes, test scenarios, aur mock requirements cover kare. Aakhir mein, main saare transport objects list karta hoon taaki full scope development start hone se pehle clear ho. Main TS ko functional consultant ke saath validate karta hoon development start karne se pehle — yeh kisi bhi cheez se zyada rework prevent karta hai.

commonMistakesSection.mistake: Forcing a pattern onto every requirement.
commonMistakesSection.whyWrong: Production impact: incorrect results or wasted effort that shows up only in QA/UAT.
commonMistakesSection.correctApproach: Not every business rule needs a design pattern. Simple IF-ELSEIF logic with two conditions is fine — Factory pattern for two conditions is over-engineering. Apply patterns when the problem has genuine variation, extensibility, or reuse requirements.

commonMistakesSection.mistake: Using generic types in interface method signatures — 'iv_data TYPE string' or 'iv_amount TYPE p'.
commonMistakesSection.whyWrong: Production impact: incorrect results or wasted effort that shows up only in QA/UAT.
commonMistakesSection.correctApproach: Use specific SAP standard types like netwr, matnr, kunnr, bukrs. Generic types require callers to handle conversion and create implicit coupling to the caller's data model.

commonMistakesSection.mistake: Defining class boundaries based on code size rather than responsibility. 'This class is getting long so let's split it' is not a valid OO design reason.
commonMistakesSection.whyWrong: Production impact: incorrect results or wasted effort that shows up only in QA/UAT.
commonMistakesSection.correctApproach: Split classes when responsibilities differ, not when code length grows. Long single-responsibility class is better than short multi-responsibility class.

commonMistakesSection.mistake: Designing one generic exception class for all errors in a domain — ZCX_PRICING_ERROR used for every pricing failure without subclasses.
commonMistakesSection.whyWrong: Production impact: incorrect results or wasted effort that shows up only in QA/UAT.
commonMistakesSection.correctApproach: This forces callers to inspect exception attributes to determine the actual error type — error-prone and fragile. Specific exception subclasses give callers clean catch blocks with clear semantics.

wordByWordSamjho.meaning: TS step 1 — interface designed first from FS requirements.

wordByWordSamjho.keyword: IMPORTING iv_amount TYPE netwr iv_currency TYPE waers
wordByWordSamjho.meaning: Method signature carries the FS-required inputs (amount, currency).
Single functional output — matches the FS-described tax result.
Each country-variant concrete class signs the same contract.
Concrete classes sealed — extension is a new class, not a subclass.
TS step 3 — selection logic via factory keyed on company code.
Selection key — company code chosen as variant driver (India vs Germany).
Standard SAP currency-key DDIC type.
Typed exception listed in TS Transport Objects — completes the design.

ooabap-034.quickRevisionNotes:
What: FS to TS conversion for OOABAP — translate business spec (multi-currency tax) into interface plus concrete classes plus factory plus transport list.

Used for:
- Producing TS deliverables that pass architect review
- Tracing each FS line item to a class/method/exception
- Listing transport objects upfront so SE10 release is clean

Key syntax / tables / Tcodes:
- TS Step 1: ZIF_TAX_CALCULATOR with IMPORTING iv_amount/iv_currency
- TS Step 2: ZCL_GST_CALCULATOR, ZCL_VAT_CALCULATOR concrete classes
- TS Step 3: ZCL_TAX_FACTORY with CLASS-METHODS create keyed on BUKRS
- TS Transport Objects: interface, concretes, factory, ZCX_UNKNOWN_BUKRS
- SE24/SE80 to build; SATC variant referenced in TS

Interview keyword: FS asks what, TS shows how — interface, concretes, factory, transport.

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you identify which design pattern to use when reading a FS?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Main FS ko specific indicators ke liye padh ke pattern candidates identify karta hoon. 'X ke based, Y ya Z karo' algorithm variation ke liye Strategy pattern indicate karta hai. 'New types add karna existing code change kiye bina hona chahiye' Factory pattern indicate karta hai. 'Jab event A occur kare, B aur C aur D zaroor hone chahiye' Observer indicate karta hai. 'Poori application mein sirf ek instance chahiye' Singleton indicate karta hai. 'Existing processor mein audit trail ya logging add karo' Decorator indicate karta hai. 'Callers ke liye complex multi-step process simplify karo' Facade indicate karta hai. Main TS mein pattern decisions rationale ke saath document karta hoon — explain karna ki har pattern kyun chose kiya reviewers ko design validate karne mein help karta hai.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: I identify pattern candidates by reading FS for specific indicators. 'Based on X, do Y or Z' indicates Strategy pattern for algorithm variation. 'Adding new types should not require changing existing code' indicates Factory pattern. 'When event A occurs, B and C and D must happen' indicates Observer. 'Only one instance needed throughout the application' indicates Singleton. 'Add audit trail or logging to existing processor' indicates Decorator. 'Simplify complex multi-step process for callers' indicates Facade. I document pattern decisions in the TS with rationale — explaining why each pattern was chosen helps reviewers validate the design.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: FS mein pattern identification skill develop karne ka best way: existing project ke code mein patterns dhundho. SE80 mein class browser open karo, naming patterns dekho — ZCL_*_FACTORY, ZCL_*_STRATEGY, ZCL_*_OBSERVER. Inhe manually trace karo. Pattern recognition is a learned skill — improves with practice.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Forcing a pattern onto every requirement. Not every business rule needs a design pattern. Simple IF-ELSEIF logic with two conditions is fine — Factory pattern for two conditions is over-engineering. Apply patterns when the problem has genuine variation, extensibility, or reuse requirements.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Pattern identification from FS keywords:
" FS: 'Dunning logic varies by customer overdue days'
"   → KEYWORD: varies → Strategy pattern
"   → ZIF_DUNNING_STRATEGY interface
"   → ZCL_DUNNING_LEVEL1, ZCL_DUNNING_LEVEL2

" FS: 'When order is saved, send email AND trigger workflow'
"   → KEYWORD: AND (multiple reactions) → Observer
"   → ZIF_ORDER_OBSERVER interface
"   → ZCL_EMAIL_OBSERVER, ZCL_WORKFLOW_OBSERVER

" FS: 'Config data read once — must be available everywhere'
"   → KEYWORD: once, everywhere → Singleton
"   → ZCL_CONFIG_MANAGER with CLASS-DATA mo_instance

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you write the interface section of a TS for OOABAP?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: OOABAP TS ke interface section mein shaamil hai: ZIF_ convention follow karne wala interface name, ek one-line purpose statement, generic types ke bajaye specific SAP data types use karte hue fully-typed parameters ke saath saare method signatures, har parameter ke liye business semantics, expected implementing classes, aur woh classes jo interface ko dependency ke roop mein use karengi. Main yeh bhi document karta hoon ki interface intentionally kya nahi include karta hai — explicit scope boundaries development ke dauraan scope creep rokte hain. Example: ZIF_TAX_CALCULATOR — tax amount calculate karta hai. Results persist nahi karta, input data validate nahi karta, currency conversion handle nahi karta.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: The interface section of an OOABAP TS includes: interface name following ZIF_ convention, a one-line purpose statement, all method signatures with fully-typed parameters using specific SAP data types rather than generic types, business semantics for each parameter, expected implementing classes, and classes that will use the interface as dependency. I also document what the interface intentionally does NOT include — explicit scope boundaries prevent scope creep during development. For example: ZIF_TAX_CALCULATOR — calculates tax amount. Does not persist results, does not validate input data, does not handle currency conversion.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Interface TS review tip: show the interface TS to a developer who did not write it and ask them to implement it from the spec alone. If they have questions about semantics — the spec is incomplete. A complete interface spec should be unambiguous enough for independent implementation.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Using generic types in interface method signatures — 'iv_data TYPE string' or 'iv_amount TYPE p'. Use specific SAP standard types like netwr, matnr, kunnr, bukrs. Generic types require callers to handle conversion and create implicit coupling to the caller's data model.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" TS Interface Section Template:
" Interface: ZIF_PRICING_ENGINE
" Purpose: Calculate net price for a material-customer combination
" Scope: Price calculation only. Does NOT include:
"   - Tax calculation (see ZIF_TAX_CALCULATOR)
"   - Discount authorization (see ZIF_DISCOUNT_AUTH)

" Method signatures:
INTERFACE zif_pricing_engine.
  METHODS get_net_price
    IMPORTING
      iv_matnr   TYPE matnr    " material number
      iv_kunnr   TYPE kunnr    " customer number
      iv_menge   TYPE menge_d  " quantity
      iv_vkorg   TYPE vkorg    " sales org
    RETURNING VALUE(rv_price) TYPE netwr
    RAISING zcx_material_not_found
            zcx_no_pricing_condition.
ENDINTERFACE.

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you explain class boundary decisions in a TS review?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Main Single Responsibility Principle use karke class boundary decisions justify karta hoon: har class ke paas exactly ek reason hota hai change hone ka. TS review mein, main boundaries aise explain karta hoon: 'ZCL_ORDER_VALIDATOR tab change hota hai jab validation business rules change hote hain. ZCL_PRICING_ENGINE tab change hota hai jab pricing algorithm change hota hai. Yeh business mein independent change reasons hain, isliye design mein yeh separate classes hain.' Main testability benefit bhi explain karta hoon: har class apni mock dependencies ke saath independently unit tested ki ja sakti hai. Aur extensibility: ek new validation rule ya new pricing algorithm add karna sirf respective class ko affect karta hai, doosre ko nahi. Agar reviewers boundary question karte hain, main poochta hoon: 'Kya in dono ko simultaneously change karne ka cause banega?' Agar answer 'different things' hai, boundary correct hai.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: I justify class boundary decisions using Single Responsibility Principle: each class has exactly one reason to change. In TS review, I explain boundaries as: 'ZCL_ORDER_VALIDATOR changes when validation business rules change. ZCL_PRICING_ENGINE changes when pricing algorithm changes. These are independent change reasons in the business, so they are separate classes in the design.' I also explain testability benefit: each class can be independently unit tested with its own mock dependencies. And extensibility: adding a new validation rule or new pricing algorithm affects only the respective class, not the other. If reviewers question a boundary, I ask: 'What would cause both of these to change simultaneously?' If the answer is 'different things,' the boundary is correct.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Common TS review debate: 'Should factory logic be in a separate class or inside the base class?' Answer: separate factory class is always cleaner. Factory class has one responsibility (selection), base class has another (interface definition or shared logic). Mixing them violates SRP and makes testing harder — factory cannot be tested independently.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Defining class boundaries based on code size rather than responsibility. 'This class is getting long so let's split it' is not a valid OO design reason. Split classes when responsibilities differ, not when code length grows. Long single-responsibility class is better than short multi-responsibility class.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" TS Review: Class boundary justification
" Question: Why ZCL_ORDER_VALIDATOR and ZCL_ORDER_PROCESSOR separate?

" Answer in TS notes:
" ZCL_ORDER_VALIDATOR:
"   Changes when: business validation rules change (e.g.,
"   new mandatory field, new customer credit check)
"   Does NOT change when: order processing logic changes

" ZCL_ORDER_PROCESSOR:
"   Changes when: order processing workflow changes (e.g.,
"   new approval step, new integration call)
"   Does NOT change when: validation rules change

" Independence confirmed → two classes justified by SRP
" Test benefit: each testable independently with mock inputs
" Extensibility: add new validation → only validator changes

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you document exception handling strategy in an OOABAP TS?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: OOABAP TS ke exception handling section mein, main exception class hierarchy define karta hoon: CX_STATIC_CHECK inherit karne wali ek base domain exception, har error type ke liye specific subclasses ke saath. TS mein har method signature apni RAISING clause include karti hai jisme list hota hai ki yeh kaunse exceptions throw kar sakti hai. Main caller contract document karta hoon: caller ko kaunse exceptions catch karne padte hain aur kya karna chahiye — user ko display karo, log karo aur retry karo, ya aage propagate karo. Har exception class design meaningful attributes include karta hai: ZCX_MATERIAL_NOT_FOUND ke liye, mv_matnr attribute material number store karta hai taaki caller helpful message display kar sake. Main CX_ROOT inheritance avoid karta hoon — saare custom exceptions compile-time checking ke liye CX_STATIC_CHECK use karte hain.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: In the OOABAP TS exception handling section, I define the exception class hierarchy: one base domain exception inheriting CX_STATIC_CHECK, with specific subclasses for each error type. Each method signature in the TS includes its RAISING clause listing which exceptions it can throw. I document the caller contract: which exceptions the caller must catch and what it should do — display to user, log and retry, or propagate further. Each exception class design includes meaningful attributes: for ZCX_MATERIAL_NOT_FOUND, the mv_matnr attribute stores the material number so the caller can display a helpful message. I avoid CX_ROOT inheritance — all custom exceptions use CX_STATIC_CHECK for compile-time checking.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Exception hierarchy depth: keep it at two levels maximum — base domain exception and specific subclasses. Three or more levels of exception hierarchy is over-engineering and makes catch blocks unnecessarily complex. Caller can catch base exception for general handling or specific subclass for targeted recovery.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Designing one generic exception class for all errors in a domain — ZCX_PRICING_ERROR used for every pricing failure without subclasses. This forces callers to inspect exception attributes to determine the actual error type — error-prone and fragile. Specific exception subclasses give callers clean catch blocks with clear semantics.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" TS Exception Design:
" Base exception:
CLASS zcx_pricing_error DEFINITION
  INHERITING FROM cx_static_check PUBLIC.
  PUBLIC SECTION.
    METHODS constructor IMPORTING iv_matnr TYPE matnr.
    DATA mv_matnr TYPE matnr READ-ONLY.
ENDCLASS.
" Specific subclasses:
CLASS zcx_material_not_found DEFINITION
  INHERITING FROM zcx_pricing_error PUBLIC.
ENDCLASS.
CLASS zcx_no_pricing_condition DEFINITION
  INHERITING FROM zcx_pricing_error PUBLIC.
ENDCLASS.
" Method contract:
" get_net_price RAISING zcx_material_not_found
"                       zcx_no_pricing_condition
" Caller catches specific → targeted recovery possible

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you estimate development effort from an OOABAP TS?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Main OOABAP development effort TS object count se estimate karta hoon: har interface definition review sahit lagbhag 2 hours leta hai. Har implementing class implementation, unit tests, aur ATC sahit 4-6 hours leti hai. Factory classes 3-4 hours leti hain. Exception classes har ek 1 hour leti hain. Integration testing aur QAS sign-off complexity ke depend 4-8 hours add karta hai. Ek typical factory pattern implementation ke liye — ek interface, do concrete classes, ek factory, do exception classes — estimate testing aur transport sahit lagbhag 20-25 hours hai. Main TS review iterations aur unexpected complexity ke liye hamesha 20% buffer add karta hoon.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: I estimate OOABAP development effort from the TS object count: each interface definition takes about 2 hours including review. Each implementing class takes 4-6 hours including implementation, unit tests, and ATC. Factory classes take 3-4 hours. Exception classes take 1 hour each. Integration testing and QAS sign-off adds 4-8 hours depending on complexity. For a typical factory pattern implementation — one interface, two concrete classes, one factory, two exception classes — the estimate is roughly 20-25 hours including testing and transport. I always add 20% buffer for TS review iterations and unexpected complexity.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Unit test writing time is the most underestimated component in OO estimates. Inexperienced developers say 'coding takes 4 hours' and forget that unit tests for that code take 2-3 additional hours. Always estimate: coding time + unit test time + ATC run and fix time. This is the honest OO estimate.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Estimating OOABAP effort the same as procedural ABAP effort. OO has higher upfront cost — interface design, class hierarchy decisions, unit test writing. The payback is in future maintenance. Never estimate OO as equivalent to procedural for same functional scope — it is typically 30-40% more upfront for first implementation in a domain.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" OOABAP Effort Estimation from TS:
" TS for Tax Calculator (Factory Pattern):
" Object                        | Effort
" ZIF_TAX_CALCULATOR (interface)| 2h design + 1h review = 3h
" ZCL_GST_CALCULATOR (class)    | 3h code + 2h unit test = 5h
" ZCL_VAT_CALCULATOR (class)    | 3h code + 2h unit test = 5h
" ZCL_TAX_FACTORY (factory)     | 3h code + 1h unit test = 4h
" ZCX_UNKNOWN_COUNTRY (exc)     | 1h = 1h
" Integration test               | 3h
" ATC + transport + QAS          | 4h
" TOTAL                         | 25h
" With 20% buffer: 30h = ~4 developer days

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you handle FS ambiguity during OOABAP TS creation?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: OOABAP TS creation mein FS ambiguity especially risky hai kyunki interface design saari implementing classes ke liye contract commit karta hai — interface mein ek wrong assumption ka matlab saari implementations mein rework. Mera process: main TS creation ke dauraan har assumption list karta hoon jo karta hoon aur unhe explicitly flag karta hoon. TS finalize karne se pehle, main functional consultant ke saath ek 30-minute walkthrough schedule karta hoon saari assumptions confirm karne ke liye. Confirmed assumptions date aur attendee names ke saath TS mein documented decisions ban jaati hain. Rejected assumptions development shuru hone se pehle TS revision trigger karti hain. Yeh front-loaded clarification development ya testing ke dauraan expensive rework rokta hai.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: FS ambiguity in OOABAP TS is especially risky because interface design commits the contract for all implementing classes — a wrong assumption in the interface means rework across all implementations. My process: I list every assumption I make during TS creation and flag them explicitly. Before finalizing the TS, I schedule a 30-minute walkthrough with the functional consultant to confirm all assumptions. Confirmed assumptions become documented decisions in the TS with the date and attendee names. Rejected assumptions trigger TS revision before development starts. This front-loaded clarification prevents expensive rework during development or testing.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Most expensive bug in software projects: wrong interface design discovered after all implementing classes are built. Agar interface mein ek return parameter add karna pada late mein — ABAP mein sab implementing classes update karni padti hain. Backward compatibility interfaces mein limited hai. Front-load your clarifications — ek ghante ki walkthrough session teen din ka rework bachata hai.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Making silent assumptions in OO TS without documenting them. Six weeks later when the assumption proves wrong, nobody remembers what was assumed and why. Document every assumption, make it visible, and get it confirmed. Silent assumptions are technical debt waiting to become expensive bugs.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" TS Assumption Documentation:
" Section: Open Questions and Assumptions
"
" CONFIRMED (walkthrough 2026-05-28, FC: Priya Sharma):
" [1] Tax is always calculated on net amount before discount
"     Interface parameter: iv_amount = net amount after header
"     discount but before item discount
" [2] Only IN and DE countries in scope for phase 1
"     Factory will raise ZCX_UNKNOWN_COUNTRY for others
"
" OPEN (pending FC response):
" [3] Should tax calculation support partial quantities?
"     Currently interface assumes full order quantity
"     → Resolution expected by 2026-06-03
"     → Development for this method blocked until resolved

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you review another developer's OOABAP TS for quality?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Doosre developer ka OOABAP TS quality ke liye review karte waqt, main aath specific quality criteria check karta hoon: interfaces generic strings ke bajaye specific SAP types use karte hain, method signatures complete RAISING clauses include karte hain, har class ke paas clearly stated single responsibility hai, dependency graph mein koi circular dependencies nahi hain, factory return types concrete classes ke bajaye interfaces hain, exception classes CX_STATIC_CHECK se inherit karti hain, unit test design happy path aur kam se kam do edge cases aur ek error path cover karta hai, aur transport objects list complete hai aur design mein mentioned saare objects se match karti hai. Main feedback numbered items ke roop mein specific suggestions ke saath provide karta hoon, general comments nahi — 'method iv_data TYPE string ko TYPE netwr hona chahiye' 'improve type specificity' ke bajaye.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: When reviewing another developer's OOABAP TS, I check eight specific quality criteria: interfaces use specific SAP types not generic strings, method signatures include complete RAISING clauses, each class has a clearly stated single responsibility, dependency graph has no circular dependencies, factory return types are interfaces not concrete classes, exception classes inherit from CX_STATIC_CHECK, unit test design covers happy path and at least two edge cases and one error path, and the transport objects list is complete and matches all objects mentioned in the design. I provide feedback as numbered items with specific suggestions, not general comments — 'method iv_data TYPE string should be TYPE netwr' rather than 'improve type specificity.'
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: TS review as a structured quality gate is underutilized in most SAP teams. Teams that skip TS review face higher rework rates. A 1-hour TS review can save 2-3 days of rework during development or testing. Invest in TS review — it is the highest ROI quality activity in OO development.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Reviewing TS only for business completeness — 'does this cover all functional requirements' — without reviewing technical design quality. Business completeness is the functional consultant's review. Developer TS review must cover technical correctness: OO design principles, type safety, exception handling, testability, and transport completeness.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" TS Review Checklist (OO-specific):
" [ ] Interface method signatures use specific SAP types
"     BAD: iv_id TYPE string
"     GOOD: iv_matnr TYPE matnr

" [ ] Factory return type is interface, not concrete class
"     BAD: RETURNING VALUE(ro) TYPE REF TO zcl_gst_calc
"     GOOD: RETURNING VALUE(ro) TYPE REF TO zif_tax_calc

" [ ] Exception classes inherit CX_STATIC_CHECK (not CX_ROOT)

" [ ] Each class has one stated responsibility

" [ ] No circular dependencies in dependency map

" [ ] Unit test scenarios: min 3 (happy, edge, error)

" [ ] Transport objects list: all ZIF_*, ZCL_*, ZCX_* listed

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you convert a FS requirement for audit logging into an OOABAP TS?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Saari pricing calculations audit-log karne ke ek FS requirement ke liye, main Decorator pattern identify karta hoon: existing pricing engine class ko modify kiye bina logging behavior add karo. TS ZCL_AUDIT_LOGGING_PRICER define karta hai jo ZIF_PRICING_ENGINE implement karta hai, real pricing engine constructor injection ke through receive karta hai. Decorator real engine call karne se pehle log karta hai: user, material, quantity, timestamp custom table ZPRICE_AUDIT_LOG ya application log mein. Delegation ke baad, yeh calculated price aur execution duration log karta hai. TS logging failure strategy bhi specify karta hai — business requirement clarification needed: agar audit log write fail hota hai, kya pricing calculation phir bhi proceed karna chahiye? Yeh ek business decision hai jo implementation se pehle FS mein confirm karna padta hai.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: For a FS requirement to audit-log all pricing calculations, I identify the Decorator pattern: add logging behavior without modifying the existing pricing engine class. The TS defines ZCL_AUDIT_LOGGING_PRICER implementing ZIF_PRICING_ENGINE, receiving the real pricing engine via constructor injection. The decorator logs before calling the real engine: user, material, quantity, timestamp to custom table ZPRICE_AUDIT_LOG or application log. After delegation, it logs the calculated price and execution duration. The TS also specifies the logging failure strategy — business requirement clarification needed: if the audit log write fails, should the pricing calculation still proceed? This is a business decision that must be confirmed in the FS before implementation.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Logging failure strategy is an underspecified business requirement in most FS documents. In financial audit scenarios, logging failure should fail the business operation — incomplete audit trail is a compliance violation. In operational logging scenarios, log failure should not block business operation — log the logging failure separately. Always clarify this from business before TS finalization.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Adding logging directly into the ZCL_PRICING_ENGINE class — this violates SRP (pricing class now has two responsibilities: pricing and logging) and Open-Closed Principle (modifying an existing class rather than extending via Decorator). The Decorator pattern keeps each class focused and extensible.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" TS: Audit Logging via Decorator Pattern
" ZCL_AUDIT_LOGGING_PRICER: implements ZIF_PRICING_ENGINE
" Constructor: io_real_pricer TYPE REF TO ZIF_PRICING_ENGINE

CLASS zcl_audit_logging_pricer DEFINITION PUBLIC FINAL.
  PUBLIC SECTION.
    INTERFACES zif_pricing_engine.
    METHODS constructor
      IMPORTING io_real TYPE REF TO zif_pricing_engine.
  PRIVATE SECTION.
    DATA mo_real TYPE REF TO zif_pricing_engine.
ENDCLASS.
CLASS zcl_audit_logging_pricer IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD zif_pricing_engine~get_net_price.
    " Log request
    INSERT zprice_audit_log FROM VALUE #(
      matnr = iv_matnr  kunnr = iv_kunnr
      uname = sy-uname  tstmp = utclong_current( ) ).
    " Delegate to real engine
    rv_price = mo_real->get_net_price(
      iv_matnr = iv_matnr  iv_kunnr = iv_kunnr
      iv_menge = iv_menge  iv_vkorg = iv_vkorg ).
    " Log result
    UPDATE zprice_audit_log SET price = rv_price
      WHERE matnr = iv_matnr AND uname = sy-uname.
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.
